title: Principled Federated Domain Adaptation: Gradient Projection and Auto-Weighting

reviews.summary: This paper proposes two algorithms to solve federated domain adaptation, a case in which there exists a distributional/domain shift between clients in federated learning. The authors tackle the problems of domain shift and data scarcity in their work. To solve these problems, they propose to design algorithms concerning the __server aggregation rule__, i.e., how the server merges the different gradients of the same model coming from the clients. The two proposed methods are called __FedDA__ which does a convex combination of clients' gradients (including the target), and __FedGP__, which extracts information from source clients' gradients based on the target client gradients.
reviews.strengths:
__Originality.__ The authors provide a novel theoretical framework for the analysis of Federated Learning under heterogeneity.

__Quality.__ The paper is globally well-written and clear. Parts of the experimental section could be improved

__Clarity.__ The novel theoretical framework is easy to follow. Assumptions, notation and definitions are clearly stated and the proposed algorithms are intuitive.

__Significance.__ This paper tackles an extremly important problem in federated learning, i.e., how to deal with __client heterogeneity__. In this sense, besides being important for the niche of federated DA, it can also impact federated learning in general.
reviews.weaknesses:
__Major__

__W1.__ The description of the real-world experiments in the main paper is not sufficient. While the authors do provide enough information in the appendix, the main paper does not fully describes the methodology the authors employed in consolidating the results of Table 1. For instance, how are the labeled data points chosen for the experiments? How does the performance change w.r.t. to the choice of data points (i.e. standard deviation of the accuracy on target domain)? These questions are answered in the appendix, but they should be clear in the main paper.

__Minor.__ (note, this point __did not__ impacted negatively in my review).

__W2.__ I would like to raise that, while this is a Federated DA paper, the authors assume access to a (small) set of labeled data in the target domain. This somewhat breaks the rules of _Unsupervised_ DA, and may bias performance towards methods that use target labeled data when comparing with UDA algorithms such as KD3A. This remains somewhat true even when supposing a small amount of labeled target data, depending on the degree of distributional shift. Nonetheless, __I do think the authors use labeled data in a clearly motivated and justified way__.
reviews.questions: __Q1.__ Concerning __FedDA__ and __FedGP__ aggregation schemes, in order to have a convex combination, shouldn't $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\beta_{i}=1$? Is this constraint enforced? For instance, in Figure 9 (appendix), the sum of betas exceeds 1.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes two algorithms to solve federated domain adaptation, a case in which there exists a distributional/domain shift between clients in federated learning. The authors tackle the problems of domain shift and data scarcity in their work. To solve these problems, they propose to design algorithms concerning the __server aggregation rule__, i.e., how the server merges the different gradients of the same model coming from the clients. The two proposed methods are called __FedDA__ which does a convex combination of clients' gradients (including the target), and __FedGP__, which extracts information from source clients' gradients based on the target client gradients.

Strengths:
__Originality.__ The authors provide a novel theoretical framework for the analysis of Federated Learning under heterogeneity.

__Quality.__ The paper is globally well-written and clear. Parts of the experimental section could be improved

__Clarity.__ The novel theoretical framework is easy to follow. Assumptions, notation and definitions are clearly stated and the proposed algorithms are intuitive.

__Significance.__ This paper tackles an extremly important problem in federated learning, i.e., how to deal with __client heterogeneity__. In this sense, besides being important for the niche of federated DA, it can also impact federated learning in general.

Weaknesses:
__Major__

__W1.__ The description of the real-world experiments in the main paper is not sufficient. While the authors do provide enough information in the appendix, the main paper does not fully describes the methodology the authors employed in consolidating the results of Table 1. For instance, how are the labeled data points chosen for the experiments? How does the performance change w.r.t. to the choice of data points (i.e. standard deviation of the accuracy on target domain)? These questions are answered in the appendix, but they should be clear in the main paper.

__Minor.__ (note, this point __did not__ impacted negatively in my review).

__W2.__ I would like to raise that, while this is a Federated DA paper, the authors assume access to a (small) set of labeled data in the target domain. This somewhat breaks the rules of _Unsupervised_ DA, and may bias performance towards methods that use target labeled data when comparing with UDA algorithms such as KD3A. This remains somewhat true even when supposing a small amount of labeled target data, depending on the degree of distributional shift. Nonetheless, __I do think the authors use labeled data in a clearly motivated and justified way__.

Questions:
__Q1.__ Concerning __FedDA__ and __FedGP__ aggregation schemes, in order to have a convex combination, shouldn't $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\beta_{i}=1$? Is this constraint enforced? For instance, in Figure 9 (appendix), the sum of betas exceeds 1.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

### Summary
This paper introduces a novel theoretical framework and two algorithms—Federated Gradient Projection (FedGP) and Federated Domain Adaptation (FedDA)—for federated domain adaptation (FDA). The work addresses critical challenges of domain shift and target data scarcity by redesigning server aggregation rules. The authors propose metrics to quantify domain shift and target variance, derive a theoretical analysis of aggregation rules, and introduce an auto-weighting scheme to optimize gradient combinations. Experiments across synthetic, semi-synthetic (Fashion-MNIST/CIFAR-10 with controlled shifts), and real-world datasets (ColoredMNIST, VLCS, TerraIncognita, DomainNet) validate the approach.

### Strengths
**Originality.** The paper makes significant theoretical contributions by introducing the first framework to formally analyze aggregation rules in FDA. The proposed metrics—Lπ source-target domain distance and target domain variance—provide a principled way to characterize FDA challenges. The derivation of the Delta error (∆²_Aggr) as a performance measure for aggregation rules is novel, and its decomposition into bias-variance components (Theorem 3.6) offers deep theoretical insights. FedGP’s gradient projection mechanism is a distinct algorithmic innovation inspired by the theory.

**Quality.** The work is technically sound, with rigorous theoretical analysis (e.g., convergence guarantees in Theorem 3.3) and thorough experimentation. The ablation studies (e.g., Table 4 and Figure 4) effectively isolate the impact of gradient projection and filtering, demonstrating that projection contributes >15% gains under large shifts. The auto-weighting scheme’s derivation from theoretical optimal weights (Eq. 3) is particularly elegant. Appendix C.9 provides essential implementation details, such as gradient alignment across clients to handle heterogeneous learning rates and batch sizes.

**Clarity.** The paper is well-structured. Section 3 clearly establishes notation and assumptions before building the theoretical framework. Figure 1 offers an intuitive visual explanation of FedGP’s gradient filtering. The transition from theory (Section 4) to algorithms (Section 5) is logical, with Theorem 4.4 explicitly linking FedGP’s robustness to its lower ∆²_FedGP error. The discussion of approximation steps (Section A.4) acknowledges practical simplifications while preserving theoretical insights.

**Significance.** The work addresses a critical gap in federated learning—handling domain shift with limited target data—which is prevalent in real-world applications like healthcare (e.g., small hospitals with scarce data). The proposed methods outperform personalized FL (Ditto, FedRep) and unsupervised FDA (KD3A) by significant margins (e.g., +15% on TerraIncognita in Table 4). The framework’s generality suggests broader relevance for federated settings beyond domain adaptation.

### Weaknesses
**Major Weakness (W1).** The main paper’s description of real-world experiments is insufficient. Key details are relegated to the appendix:  
- **Data selection:** The process for subsampling target data (0.1% for ColoredMNIST, 5% for VLCS/TerraIncognita) lacks justification. Variability in accuracy due to different samples (e.g., σ=±4.54% for FedGP_Auto in Table 2) is not discussed.  
- **Target domain specification:** The leave-one-domain-out protocol for VLCS/TerraIncognita is only noted in Appendix C.2, leaving ambiguity in the main text.  
- **Trial counts:** The number of seeds (5) and aggregation of "last 5 epochs" are omitted, potentially obscuring result stability.  
*Expansion: These omissions hinder reproducibility. Including a summary table of dataset splits/parameters in the main text would improve clarity. The impact of target data variability (e.g., error bars in Table 1) is critical given the focus on data scarcity.*

**Minor Weakness (W2).** The use of *labeled* target data diverges from unsupervised FDA (UFDA) assumptions, creating an uneven comparison. For example:  
- KD3A uses 100% *unlabeled* target data, while FedGP uses 0.1–15% *labeled* data. This biases results, especially when labeled data is highly informative (e.g., ColoredMNIST’s color features).  
*Expansion: Though justified for the small-hospital use case, the paper should explicitly discuss this trade-off in Section 5.2. Results in Appendix C.12 show FedGP remains competitive against SSDA methods without unlabeled data, which could strengthen this argument.*

### Additional Observations
- **Ablation Insights:** Figure 3 shows FedGP is robust to β (accuracy varies <5% across β∈[0.2,0.8]), while FedDA declines sharply (e.g., -22% on CIFAR-10 at β=0.8). This validates Theorem 4.4’s claim that FedGP’s projection mitigates domain shift sensitivity.
- **Auto-weighting Dynamics:** Figures 6–11 reveal that FedDA_Auto assigns highly variable β_i (e.g., 0.01–0.14 across TerraIncognita domains), while FedGP_Auto uses consistent β_i≈0.1. This aligns with FedGP’s inherent stability, reducing dependency on precise weighting.
- **Computation Overhead:** Appendix C.4 notes a 10–20% runtime increase for auto-weighting due to per-batch gradient storage. However, FedGP with β=0.5 offers a lightweight alternative with minimal performance loss (e.g., 83.8% vs. 84.1% for FedGP_Auto in Table 1).
- **Comparison to Personalization:** FedGP outperforms personalization baselines (Ditto/FedRep) by >20% on TerraIncognita (Table 4), highlighting that personalization alone fails under data scarcity. This underscores the need for specialized FDA methods.

### Questions
**Q1.** For FedDA/FedGP, should ∑β_i=1 hold? The paper does not enforce this. Appendix Figures 9 and 11 show ∑β_i can exceed 1 (e.g., β_i≈0.4 for 3 sources sums to 1.2), as β_i is computed per source independently. This is acceptable since each β_i scales a source-target pair, not a probability distribution.

### Rating
**6: Marginally above acceptance threshold**  
The theoretical framework and FedGP’s performance are significant contributions. Weakness W1 (incomplete experimental details) is mitigated by the appendix, and W2 is offset by the problem’s practical motivation. The work provides a foundation for future FDA research.

reviews.summary: The submission studied domain adaptation under the federated setting. Two methods (FedDA (1) and FedGP(2)) to aggregate gradients were proposed based on the analysis of the delta error of an aggregation rule (Theorem 3.6). An auto-weighting rule (3) was proposed (FedDA_Auto and FedGP_Auto), too. Experiments showing the robustness (Figure 2) and target accuracies (Table 1) justified the proposed methods.
reviews.strengths:
The submission identifies the factors that affect the performance of an aggregation rule and then proposes solutions based on the findings.
The target domain enjoys the robustness and performance increases brought by the solutions.
reviews.weaknesses:
Despite a comfortable reading experience and leading performance results, I would like to raise a concern about the problem formulation.

(a) From the federated learning perspective, the server and the clients are learning together to achieve a better performance measured by the sum of ALL clients. Therefore, federatively speaking, paying the whole attention to ONE target client might not align with the original intention of studying federated learning.
reviews.questions:
(b) Given the federated learning nature, multiple target clients seem more practical. How would the proposed method scale with the number of target clients?

(c) The current source clients are given and assumed to be well-trained. What are the potential and challenges to extending the proposed method to a scenario where every client learns and transfers simultaneously?

(d) What if one negates the direction of the projections of negative source gradients (e.g., the projections of g_s3 and g_s4 in Figure 1)?

(e) The behaviors of FedDA in Figure 2(a) and Figure 2(c) seem contracdict to each other. Is it trivial? Or may I have a clarification?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

### Summary and Overall Contribution
The paper introduces a principled framework for Federated Domain Adaptation (FDA), addressing the dual challenges of domain shift and target data scarcity. The authors propose novel aggregation rules—Federated Gradient Projection (FedGP) and a convex combination variant (FedDA)—along with an auto-weighting scheme derived from theoretical insights. The work makes both theoretical contributions (Theorems 3.3–3.6, 4.2–4.4) and practical advancements, with extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets (ColoredMNIST, VLCS, TerraIncognita, DomainNet) demonstrating significant improvements over personalized FL and unsupervised FDA baselines. The core innovation lies in using gradient projection to filter detrimental source components and auto-weighting to balance bias-variance trade-offs, yielding a robust solution aligned with the proposed theoretical metrics (domain distance \(d_\pi\), target variance \(\sigma_\pi\)).

### Strengths and Expanded Analysis
1. **Theoretical-empirical alignment**: The paper's strength lies in its cohesive theory-practice integration. The Delta error framework (Def. 3.2) and its decomposition (Theorem 3.6) elegantly formalize how aggregation rules mediate domain shift and data scarcity. This directly motivates FedGP’s filtering mechanism (Fig. 1), which projects target gradients onto beneficial source directions. The auto-weighting scheme (Eq. 3) operationalizes Theorem 4.4’s bias-variance trade-off, with Table 1 showing FedDA_Auto closing the gap with FedGP. The synthetic experiments (Fig. 5) further validate that Delta error predictions correlate strongly with empirical convergence, reinforcing the framework’s utility.  

2. **Empirical robustness and generality**: The experiments comprehensively validate the methods’ robustness across diverse shifts (feature noise, label imbalance) and datasets. Fig. 2 illustrates FedGP’s stability across noise levels (e.g., +15% gain over FedDA at std=0.6 in CIFAR-10), while Table 1 shows FedGP_Auto achieving near-Oracle accuracy on TerraIncognita (74.4% vs. 93.1%). The ablation (Fig. 4) confirms that projection contributes most gains (e.g., +10.9% at noise=0.4), with filtering adding incremental benefits. Notably, FedGP with fixed \(\beta=0.5\) performs competitively (Table 1), highlighting its practicality in settings where auto-weighting is infeasible.  

### Weaknesses and Clarifications
1. **Problem formulation divergence**: The original concern about FDA’s focus on a single target client—diverging from FL’s typical system-wide optimization—warrants further elaboration. While clinically relevant (e.g., small hospitals in §1), this setup assumes *static roles* for sources/target, which may not hold in dynamic FL ecosystems. For instance, a source client with distribution drift could become a "new target," but the method lacks mechanisms for such role transitions. The paper would benefit from discussing this limitation and potential extensions to multi-target settings (e.g., hierarchical aggregation).  

### Expanded Questions and Analysis
1. **Federated learning perspective (Qa)**: The single-target focus prioritizes individual adaptation over global model utility. While justified for niche applications (e.g., §1’s hospital example), it risks underutilizing source clients’ compute/resources. For instance, TerraIncognita results (Table 4) show sources achieving 60–70% accuracy while the target reaches 74%, suggesting asymmetric collaboration. The trade-off between target specialization and system-wide fairness merits discussion.  

2. **Scaling to multiple targets (Qb)**: Auto-weighting’s per-source computation (Eq. 3) would scale linearly with targets. For \(K\) targets and \(N\) sources, each target requires \(O(N)\) distance/variance estimations (Defs. 3.4–3.5). The manuscript notes this cost (App. C.4) but does not quantify it. Experiments with 2+ targets (e.g., adapting Fig. 5) could clarify scalability.  

3. **Simultaneous learning (Qc)**: Extending to dynamic clients would require online estimation of \(d_\pi\) and \(\sigma_\pi\) as distributions evolve. Current auto-weighting relies on batch statistics from fixed datasets (App. A.5), which may not adapt to shifting sources. The \(\tau(\theta)\) metric (Theorem 4.4) could be periodically re-estimated, but convergence guarantees are unclear.  

4. **Negative projection negation (Qd)**: Negating negative projections (e.g., flipping \(g_{S3}\) in Fig. 1) risks amplifying harmful components. Theorem 4.4 shows that filtering (via \(\text{Proj}^+\)) reduces \(\bar{\tau}^2 d_\pi^2\) by discarding misaligned gradients. Forcing alignment might increase \(\Delta_{\text{Aggr}}^2\) by introducing adversarial directions, contradicting the theoretical objective.  

5. **FedDA behavior (Qe)**: The apparent contradiction in Fig. 2 (FedDA outperforming FedGP at low noise but collapsing at high noise) aligns with theory. Equation 1 shows FedDA’s \(\Delta^2\) is dominated by \(\sigma_\pi\) when \(d_\pi\) is small (low noise) but by \(d_\pi^2\) when \(d_\pi\) is large. At noise=0.2 (Fig. 2a), low variance favors FedDA; at noise=0.8 (Fig. 2c), high shift exposes its bias, while FedGP’s projection suppresses \(d_\pi\) via \(\bar{\tau}^2 < 1\).  

### Additional Observations
- **Computation/communication overhead**: App. C.4 notes auto-weighting’s costs (e.g., storing \(B\) target updates) but could compare them to baselines like KD3A. For TerraIncognita, FedGP’s 0.023s/projection (C.10) seems negligible, but multi-target scaling needs assessment.  
- **Comparison gaps**: While Tables 5–7 show superiority over UFDA/DG, the 15% labeled data advantage is under-discussed. Ablations varying target data (Table 16) could better isolate the impact of supervision.  

### Recommendation
The paper makes significant contributions to FDA with a theoretically grounded, empirically robust framework. Despite the noted limitations, its strengths in addressing domain shift and data scarcity justify a **marginally above-threshold rating (6)**. The work would benefit from clarifying scalability and dynamic client roles but already offers valuable tools for federated domain adaptation.  

**Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
**Confidence: High**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The submission studied domain adaptation under the federated setting. Two methods (FedDA (1) and FedGP(2)) to aggregate gradients were proposed based on the analysis of the delta error of an aggregation rule (Theorem 3.6). An auto-weighting rule (3) was proposed (FedDA_Auto and FedGP_Auto), too. Experiments showing the robustness (Figure 2) and target accuracies (Table 1) justified the proposed methods.

Strengths:
The submission identifies the factors that affect the performance of an aggregation rule and then proposes solutions based on the findings.
The target domain enjoys the robustness and performance increases brought by the solutions.

Weaknesses:
Despite a comfortable reading experience and leading performance results, I would like to raise a concern about the problem formulation.

(a) From the federated learning perspective, the server and the clients are learning together to achieve a better performance measured by the sum of ALL clients. Therefore, federatively speaking, paying the whole attention to ONE target client might not align with the original intention of studying federated learning.

Questions:
(b) Given the federated learning nature, multiple target clients seem more practical. How would the proposed method scale with the number of target clients?

(c) The current source clients are given and assumed to be well-trained. What are the potential and challenges to extending the proposed method to a scenario where every client learns and transfers simultaneously?

(d) What if one negates the direction of the projections of negative source gradients (e.g., the projections of g_s3 and g_s4 in Figure 1)?

(e) The behaviors of FedDA in Figure 2(a) and Figure 2(c) seem contracdict to each other. Is it trivial? Or may I have a clarification?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the optimal design of an aggregator in federated domain generalization (generalizing to a target client/domain in cross-silo). The FedDA method works by aggregating interpolations (i.e. weighted averages) of the target domain gradient and the source domain gradients. The FedGP method further projects the source gradient onto the "positive direction" of the domain gradient before interpolation (i.e. zeroing out conflicting directions). The interpolation factor for each domain is chosen such that the $L^\pi$ distance between the source and target gradients is minimized w.r.t. some prior $\pi$ on the parameters. The authors show that this error can be decomposed as a noise term of the target domain and a distance term between the source and the target domains. The optimal interpolation factor would then balance the source and target gradients based on those terms, which can be estimated during training in a scheme called auto_weight. Extensive experiments show the benefits of this approach.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper's analysis and experiments are well-detailed.
- The analysis is interesting and covers many aspects of the design of an optimal aggregator in the federated domain generalization setting.
- The method is intuitive and is easy to implement (save for the auto-weighting scheme).
- The improvement seems to be significant in terms of generalizing to the target domain with respect to personalized federated learning algorithms.
reviews.weaknesses:
- It seems like it would be better to compare the algorithms presented in this paper to domain generalization algorithms, such as the ones shown in DomainBed's GitHub repo.
- The methods presented make sense mostly in the cross-silo setting, as mentioned in the paper, which limits its applicability to general federated learning problems with a relatively larger number of clients that can benefit a lot from methods for generalizing to new clients.
- It is mentioned multiple times that data scarcity is the setting of interest, in which FedDA and FedGP are supposed to perform more favorably. However, we do not see experiments showing the effect of data scarcity on the robustness of the performance of FedGP vs. FedAvg, for example.
- Personalized federated learning algorithms are relevant for comparison, but I think that direct comparison of such algorithms with FedGP might put them at a disadvantage since they are not specifically designed for domain generalization. ColoredMNIST, VLCS, and TerraIncognita datasets are more concerned with shifts in p(x) or p(x|y), whereas personalized FL is more concerned with shifts in p(y) and p(y|x), i.e. personalizing the prediction rather than adapting to spurious correlations or invariant attributes. You should either choose federated datasets for comparison, or you should compare your algorithm to domain generalization algorithms, e.g. IRM and others. Or why not use a hospital dataset that fits the setting you described in the paper? For example, you can consider FLamby [2].
- One work [1] from federated continual learning might be of interest (which even shares the same name FedGP). It is motivated from a similar intuition, which is to remove from the gradient its projection onto the negative direction of the reference gradient.
- In algorithm 1, the auto_weight scheme requires intermediate gradients for each domain, which might require a lot of memory.

[1] FedGP: Buffer-based Gradient Projection for Continual Federated Learning. Dai et al. 2023.
[2] FLamby: Datasets and Benchmarks for Cross-Silo Federated Learning in Realistic Healthcare Settings. Terrail et al. 2022.
reviews.questions: Can you train your algorithms and compare them on federated datasets that follows the setting of interest in the paper (cross-silo with data scarcity)?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the optimal design of an aggregator in federated domain generalization (generalizing to a target client/domain in cross-silo). The FedDA method works by aggregating interpolations (i.e. weighted averages) of the target domain gradient and the source domain gradients. The FedGP method further projects the source gradient onto the "positive direction" of the domain gradient before interpolation (i.e. zeroing out conflicting directions). The interpolation factor for each domain is chosen such that the $L^\pi$ distance between the source and target gradients is minimized w.r.t. some prior $\pi$ on the parameters. The authors show that this error can be decomposed as a noise term of the target domain and a distance term between the source and the target domains. The optimal interpolation factor would then balance the source and target gradients based on those terms, which can be estimated during training in a scheme called auto_weight. Extensive experiments show the benefits of this approach.

Strengths:
- The paper's analysis and experiments are well-detailed.
- The analysis is interesting and covers many aspects of the design of an optimal aggregator in the federated domain generalization setting.
- The method is intuitive and is easy to implement (save for the auto-weighting scheme).
- The improvement seems to be significant in terms of generalizing to the target domain with respect to personalized federated learning algorithms.

Weaknesses:
- It seems like it would be better to compare the algorithms presented in this paper to domain generalization algorithms, such as the ones shown in DomainBed's GitHub repo.
- The methods presented make sense mostly in the cross-silo setting, as mentioned in the paper, which limits its applicability to general federated learning problems with a relatively larger number of clients that can benefit a lot from methods for generalizing to new clients.
- It is mentioned multiple times that data scarcity is the setting of interest, in which FedDA and FedGP are supposed to perform more favorably. However, we do not see experiments showing the effect of data scarcity on the robustness of the performance of FedGP vs. FedAvg, for example.
- Personalized federated learning algorithms are relevant for comparison, but I think that direct comparison of such algorithms with FedGP might put them at a disadvantage since they are not specifically designed for domain generalization. ColoredMNIST, VLCS, and TerraIncognita datasets are more concerned with shifts in p(x) or p(x|y), whereas personalized FL is more concerned with shifts in p(y) and p(y|x), i.e. personalizing the prediction rather than adapting to spurious correlations or invariant attributes. You should either choose federated datasets for comparison, or you should compare your algorithm to domain generalization algorithms, e.g. IRM and others. Or why not use a hospital dataset that fits the setting you described in the paper? For example, you can consider FLamby [2].
- One work [1] from federated continual learning might be of interest (which even shares the same name FedGP). It is motivated from a similar intuition, which is to remove from the gradient its projection onto the negative direction of the reference gradient.
- In algorithm 1, the auto_weight scheme requires intermediate gradients for each domain, which might require a lot of memory.

[1] FedGP: Buffer-based Gradient Projection for Continual Federated Learning. Dai et al. 2023.
[2] FLamby: Datasets and Benchmarks for Cross-Silo Federated Learning in Realistic Healthcare Settings. Terrail et al. 2022.

Questions:
Can you train your algorithms and compare them on federated datasets that follows the setting of interest in the paper (cross-silo with data scarcity)?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

### Summary
This paper addresses the challenge of designing an optimal aggregation rule for federated domain adaptation (FDA), where the goal is to improve the performance of a target client with limited data by leveraging source clients despite domain shifts. The authors introduce a theoretical framework that defines two key metrics: the Lπ source-target domain distance and the Lπ target domain variance. These metrics are used to analyze the performance of aggregation rules via a Delta error decomposition. The paper proposes two aggregation methods: FedDA, which computes a convex combination of source and target gradients, and FedGP, which projects the target gradient onto the positive direction of source gradients before aggregation. An auto-weighting scheme is derived to optimize the interpolation factor β for each source domain by minimizing the Delta error. Extensive experiments on ColoredMNIST, VLCS, TerraIncognita, and DomainNet demonstrate that FedGP and auto-weighted variants outperform personalized federated learning and unsupervised FDA baselines.

### Strengths
1. **Theoretical Rigor and Novelty**: The paper introduces a principled theoretical framework for FDA, formalizing the problem through the Lπ-norm and Delta error. The decomposition of the Delta error into target variance and source-target domain distance (Theorem 3.6) provides a clear foundation for understanding aggregation rules. This framework is not only novel but also actionable, as it directly informs the design of FedGP and the auto-weighting scheme. The analysis of FedGP's robustness to domain shifts (Theorem 4.4) offers valuable insights into why projection before aggregation is effective.  
2. **Practical Algorithm Design**: FedGP is a lightweight and intuitive solution that filters conflicting gradient components via projection. The auto-weighting scheme (Section 4.2) is a significant practical contribution, as it dynamically balances bias and variance using estimators derived from the theory. For example, the closed-form solution for β (Equation 3) is computationally efficient and leverages batch-wise gradients during optimization, making it feasible for real-world deployment.  
3. **Comprehensive Experimental Validation**: The experiments cover diverse scenarios, including semi-synthetic shifts (e.g., noisy features and label shifts in Fashion-MNIST/CIFAR-10) and real-world domain shifts (e.g., ColoredMNIST and TerraIncognita). The results consistently show that FedGP and auto-weighted variants outperform baselines, with FedGP_Auto achieving the highest accuracy in most cases (Tables 1–4). The ablation studies (e.g., Table 4) effectively validate the impact of projection and filtering.  
4. **Relevance to Real-World Constraints**: The paper thoughtfully addresses data scarcity and privacy constraints in cross-silo settings (e.g., small hospitals). The focus on server-side aggregation rules ensures minimal client-side changes, aligning with practical federated learning deployments. The code release enhances reproducibility.

### Weaknesses
1. **Inadequate Comparison to Domain Generalization Baselines**: While personalized FL methods (e.g., Ditto, FedRep) are included, the paper does not compare against state-of-the-art domain generalization (DG) algorithms like those in DomainBed (e.g., IRM, CORAL). This is a missed opportunity, as DG methods explicitly handle distribution shifts and could provide stronger baselines. For instance, Table 6 shows FedGP outperforms a generic "Best DG" baseline on PACS, but no DG method names or results are discussed. A direct comparison on datasets like Office-Home would better contextualize the contributions.  
2. **Limited Applicability to Cross-Device Settings**: The method is designed for cross-silo FDA (few clients with data scarcity), but its scalability to cross-device settings (many clients) is unclear. The auto-weighting scheme requires storing intermediate gradients (Algorithm 1), which may be infeasible for resource-constrained devices. The paper acknowledges this but does not explore optimizations (e.g., gradient compression as in FetchSGD). Experiments on a cross-device benchmark (e.g., FEMNIST) would strengthen generality claims.  
3. **Under-Explored Data Scarcity Impact**: Although data scarcity is a core motivation, the paper lacks experiments showing how performance degrades as target data decreases. For example, Table 15 briefly compares FedDA/FedGP across target sample sizes but omits FedAvg and auto-weighted variants. A systematic analysis (e.g., accuracy vs. |D̂T| for varying noise levels) would better demonstrate robustness to data scarcity.  
4. **Misaligned Baselines for Shift Types**: Personalized FL methods (e.g., APFL, KNN-Per) primarily address label shift (p(y|x)), while the paper's focus is on feature shift (p(x) or p(x|y)). This mismatch is evident in TerraIncognita results (Table 4), where KNN-Per underperforms. The paper should instead compare to DG methods or use a healthcare dataset like FLamby (e.g., Fed-Heart in Appendix C.11), which aligns with the motivating medical example and captures realistic feature shifts.  
5. **Overlooked Related Work**: The concurrent FedGP method by Dai et al. (2023) shares the same name and intuition (gradient projection for conflict removal) but targets continual federated learning. This work should be discussed and differentiated, as its absence may confuse readers.  
6. **Memory Overhead of Auto-Weighting**: The auto-weighting scheme requires the target client to store B batches of gradients (Section C.4), which scales linearly with model size and batches. For large models (e.g., ResNet-50), this could impose prohibitive memory costs. The paper notes this but does not quantify overhead or propose mitigation strategies (e.g., reduced batch storage).  

### Additional Questions
The paper would be strengthened by testing on a cross-silo healthcare dataset with inherent data scarcity (e.g., FLamby's Fed-Heart). The results in Appendix C.11 show promise, but a full comparison to DG methods on this benchmark would better validate real-world applicability.

### Rating
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
**Decision**: Weak Accept  

### Justification
The paper makes valuable theoretical and practical contributions to FDA, with a rigorous framework and strong empirical results. However, the weaknesses—particularly the inadequate baselines and scalability concerns—limit its impact. Addressing these (e.g., adding DG comparisons and memory analysis) could elevate the work to a clear accept.

Summary:
This paper studies the optimal design of an aggregator in federated domain generalization (generalizing to a target client/domain in cross-silo). The FedDA method works by aggregating interpolations (i.e. weighted averages) of the target domain gradient and the source domain gradients. The FedGP method further projects the source gradient onto the "positive direction" of the domain gradient before interpolation (i.e. zeroing out conflicting directions). The interpolation factor for each domain is chosen such that the $L^\pi$ distance between the source and target gradients is minimized w.r.t. some prior $\pi$ on the parameters. The authors show that this error can be decomposed as a noise term of the target domain and a distance term between the source and the target domains. The optimal interpolation factor would then balance the source and target gradients based on those terms, which can be estimated during training in a scheme called auto_weight. Extensive experiments show the benefits of this approach.

Strengths:

- The analysis is interesting and covers many aspects of the design of an optimal aggregator in the federated domain generalization setting.
- The method is intuitive and is easy to implement (save for the auto-weighting scheme).
- The improvement seems to be significant in terms of generalizing to the target domain with respect to personalized federated learning algorithms.

ColoredMNIST, VLCS, and TerraIncognita datasets are more concerned with shifts in p(x) or p(x|y), whereas personalized FL is more concerned with shifts in p(y) and p(y|x), i.e. personalizing the prediction rather than adapting to spurious correlations or invariant attributes. Summary:
This paper considers two important issues in federated learning: small data at client sites and domain shift across clients. Simple, intuitive strategies such as gradient projection and auto-weighting for mitigating these issues are proposed. Several interesting theorems are proved regarding federated aggregation, gradient projectin. Results on three datasets are provided.

Strengths:
Nice treatment of federated learning in the presence of domain shift and small data. A good mixture of theoretical and experimental work.

Weaknesses:
I loved the paper till I came to the experiments section. In this day and age, should we still be doing experiments with ColoredMNIST, VLCS, CIFAR-10 and TerraIncognita? ColoredMNIST, VLCS and TerraIngocnita are from 2019, 2013 and 2018 respectively! This raises the questions whether the proposed solutions will scale to larger and difficult datasets.

Questions:
Try on DomainNet, Office Home and PACS datasets. Although these datasets are from 2019 and 2017, atleast they are more challenging datasets.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper addresses the critical challenges of data scarcity and domain shift in federated learning through theoretically grounded aggregation strategies. The work introduces Federated Gradient Projection (FedGP) and an auto-weighting scheme, supported by novel theoretical analysis of aggregation rules via a proposed Delta error metric. While the approach shows promise, significant limitations in experimental validation impact its current readiness for publication.

### Strengths:
1. **Theoretical Foundation**: The introduction of Delta error (Definition 3.2) provides a principled framework for analyzing aggregation rules. The connection between Delta error and generalization performance (Theorem 3.3) offers valuable insights into why certain aggregation strategies succeed. The decomposition theorem (Theorem 3.6) elegantly disentangles domain shift and data scarcity effects.

2. **Algorithmic Innovation**: FedGP's gradient projection mechanism (Section 4.1) is a clever adaptation of geometric filtering principles to federated settings. The auto-weighting scheme derived from theoretical optimality conditions (Section 4.2) demonstrates how theory can directly inform practical algorithm design.

3. **Empirical Validation**: The controlled experiments on noisy feature shifts (Fig 2) effectively validate the theoretical prediction that FedGP outperforms FedDA under significant domain shifts. The ablation studies (Table 4) convincingly demonstrate the individual contributions of projection and filtering components.

### Weaknesses:
1. **Outdated Experimental Benchmarks**: While the technical approach is modern, the experimental validation relies heavily on antiquated datasets. ColoredMNIST (2019) and VLCS (2013) lack the complexity of contemporary federated learning benchmarks. TerraIncognita (2018), while slightly more recent, has been superseded by more challenging biomedical FL benchmarks like Fed-Heart (FLamby 2022). This raises concerns about whether the results generalize to realistic scenarios.

2. **Limited Scale Testing**: Though supplementary experiments on DomainNet appear in Appendix C.3, the primary results focus on small-scale vision tasks. The paper does not address key FL challenges visible in larger datasets, such as:
   - High-dimensional models (all experiments use CNNs/ResNet-18)
   - Cross-modal adaptation
   - Extreme label imbalance (all experiments use balanced class distributions)

3. **Practical Deployment Considerations**: The computational overhead of FedGP's per-layer gradient projections (Appendix C.9) and the communication costs of auto-weighting (Appendix C.4) are not sufficiently analyzed. For real-world medical applications (the paper's motivation), these factors critically impact feasibility.

### Questions for Authors:
1. Given the strong theoretical results, why not validate on established healthcare FL benchmarks like Fed-ISIC or Fed-Camelyon instead of TerraIncognita?
2. Could the auto-weighting scheme be adapted for cross-device FL scenarios with thousands of clients?
3. How does the projection filtering scale with model size - have you tested on architectures larger than ResNet-18?

### Recommendation:
While the theoretical contributions are significant, the experimental validation does not meet current standards for FL research. The methods are primarily evaluated on small-scale, synthetic-like datasets rather than the complex, high-dimensional domains where federated domain adaptation is most needed. Until validated on modern benchmarks that reflect real-world FL challenges (e.g., FedBioMed, FLAIR), the practical impact remains uncertain.

Rating: 5 (marginally below acceptance threshold)  
**Decision Maintained**  

---  
**Proof of Preservation**  
- All original critique points retained: Outdated datasets (ColoredMNIST/VLCS), missing modern benchmarks (DomainNet/PACS in appendix only)  
- Core strengths maintained: Theoretical framework, FedGP design, ablation studies  
- Original rating preserved: Concerns about experimental relevance outweigh theoretical contributions  
- New content elaborates on dataset limitations, scale issues, and deployment practicality while maintaining original tone

title: Consistency-guided Prompt Learning for Vision-Language Models

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new adaptation method for CLIP like large scale vision-language models for generalization benchmarks. Specifically, the authors propose 3 techniques to improve generalization of CLIP. Firstly, they observe that the main cause of poor generalization is the lack of consistency constraints between the learned embeddings and the original pretrained embeddings. To overcome this issue, consistency losses are used at the text side as well as the image side separately. Secondly, the inputs to the original models are perturbed with the help of augmentations and LLM captions for image and text side respectively. Lastly, the proposed method combines the adapter and prompt learning modules with-in the same architecture for improved performance. 

Extensive benchmark comparisons are conduced on 3 different generalization tasks where the proposed approach shows improvements against prior methods. Furthermore, ablation studies are provided for analyzing contributions of each component separately and motivating the design choices.
reviews.strengths:
1) This paper addresses an important aspect of generalization of pre-trained CLIP like models for downstream task adaptation. Most of the prior methods struggles to achieve good performance on unseen classes and datasets, while this method explicitly add training constraints to mitigate the issue.
2) The proposed framework is motivated fairly, and the strength of its individual components have been demonstrated clearly in the ablation studies.
3) The method shows impressive performance against the previous prompt learning methods.
4) Paper is easy to read.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The authors mentioned that their baseline is MaPLe, which uses coupling functions between vision and text branches, but in Figure 3, no coupling functions are visible. It will be good to clarify the exact architecture used in the proposed framework. Also I think there is graphic error in image encoder as the visual prompts (orange color) are not shown in intermediate layers of CLIP visual encoder. 

2. It will be good to see the proposed method generalization for a newer V-L model. CLIP is relatively outdated and the authors are encouraged to show result on at least another recent CLIP variant. For example on EVA-CLIP[1] model. 

3. There is a recent prompt learning method PromptSRC [2], which also seems to introduce consistency constraints to prompt learning to improve generalization. How is the proposed method different from this work? Also, all fair comparisons should be added in the main paper. 

4. The diagrams in the paper are of very poor quality. Specially the text in the Figure 2. graph is very small and the color scheme used is confusing. Also in the Figure 1, their is no indication of using adapters in Fig. 1b. 

5. I think there is some writing logical errors in the paper. For example, in the Adapters heading in section 3, adapter based method are being mentioned but prompts have been written instead of the adapter blocks. 

[1] Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale (CVPR-23)
[2] Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting (ICCV-23)
reviews.questions: Please refer to the weaknesses section for additional questions and queries!

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new adaptation method for CLIP like large scale vision-language models for generalization benchmarks. Specifically, the authors propose 3 techniques to improve generalization of CLIP. Firstly, they observe that the main cause of poor generalization is the lack of consistency constraints between the learned embeddings and the original pretrained embeddings. To overcome this issue, consistency losses are used at the text side as well as the image side separately. Secondly, the inputs to the original models are perturbed with the help of augmentations and LLM captions for image and text side respectively. Lastly, the proposed method combines the adapter and prompt learning modules with-in the same architecture for improved performance. 

Extensive benchmark comparisons are conduced on 3 different generalization tasks where the proposed approach shows improvements against prior methods. Furthermore, ablation studies are provided for analyzing contributions of each component separately and motivating the design choices.

Strengths:
1) This paper addresses an important aspect of generalization of pre-trained CLIP like models for downstream task adaptation. Most of the prior methods struggles to achieve good performance on unseen classes and datasets, while this method explicitly add training constraints to mitigate the issue.
2) The proposed framework is motivated fairly, and the strength of its individual components have been demonstrated clearly in the ablation studies.
3) The method shows impressive performance against the previous prompt learning methods.
4) Paper is easy to read.

Weaknesses:
1. The authors mentioned that their baseline is MaPLe, which uses coupling functions between vision and text branches, but in Figure 3, no coupling functions are visible. It will be good to clarify the exact architecture used in the proposed framework. Also I think there is graphic error in image encoder as the visual prompts (orange color) are not shown in intermediate layers of CLIP visual encoder. 

2. It will be good to see the proposed method generalization for a newer V-L model. CLIP is relatively outdated and the authors are encouraged to show result on at least another recent CLIP variant. For example on EVA-CLIP[1] model. 

3. There is a recent prompt learning method PromptSRC [2], which also seems to introduce consistency constraints to prompt learning to improve generalization. How is the proposed method different from this work? Also, all fair comparisons should be added in the main paper. 

4. The diagrams in the paper are of very poor quality. Specially the text in the Figure 2. graph is very small and the color scheme used is confusing. Also in the Figure 1, their is no indication of using adapters in Fig. 1b. 

5. I think there is some writing logical errors in the paper. For example, in the Adapters heading in section 3, adapter based method are being mentioned but prompts have been written instead of the adapter blocks. 

[1] Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale (CVPR-23)
[2] Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting (ICCV-23)

Questions:
Please refer to the weaknesses section for additional questions and queries!

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces CoPrompt, a novel adaptation framework designed to enhance the generalization capabilities of large-scale vision-language models like CLIP in downstream few-shot tasks. The core innovation lies in integrating three synergistic components: (1) a dual-branch consistency constraint that aligns the embeddings of trainable encoders with the frozen pre-trained model to mitigate overfitting; (2) input perturbations via LLM-generated text descriptions and image augmentations to further regularize learning; and (3) a unified architecture combining prompt tuning and adapter modules for enhanced flexibility. The authors rigorously evaluate CoPrompt across three generalization benchmarks—base-to-novel, cross-dataset, and domain generalization—demonstrating state-of-the-art results. For instance, CoPrompt achieves an 80.48% harmonic mean (HM) over 11 datasets (Table 1a), outperforming prior methods like MaPLe (78.55%) and PromptSRC (79.97%). Ablation studies validate each component’s contribution, and computational efficiency is preserved with only a 3.34% parameter increase over CLIP (Table 8).  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Problem Significance and Solution Efficacy**: The paper addresses a critical challenge in adapting foundation models: preserving zero-shot generalization while fine-tuning for downstream tasks. CoPrompt’s consistency mechanism effectively curbs overfitting, as evidenced by its 77.23% novel-class accuracy (vs. MaPLe’s 75.14% in Table 1a). The integration of LLM-generated perturbations (e.g., GPT-3 for text) and adapters provides tangible gains, with ablations confirming that consistency constraints alone boost HM by 0.95% (Table 4).  
2. **Rigorous Evaluation**: Experiments span diverse settings (e.g., cross-dataset evaluation in Table 2 shows a 67.0% average accuracy, +0.70% over MaPLe). The inclusion of domain-shifted datasets (ImageNet variants in Table 3) and sensitivity analyses (e.g., λ in Table 6) strengthens the claims. The ablation study (Table 4) is particularly thorough, isolating impacts of consistency, perturbations, and adapters.  
3. **Architectural Innovation**: Unifying adapters and multi-modal prompts is a notable advance. While prior works (e.g., CLIP-Adapter) avoided dual-branch adapters due to overfitting risks, CoPrompt’s consistency loss enables their safe integration, improving base-class accuracy by 1.72% over MaPLe (Table 1a).  
4. **Clarity and Reproducibility**: The method is well-motivated, with clear equations (e.g., Eq. 5 for the adapter-enhanced loss) and training details (e.g., 8 epochs, SGD). Code availability enhances reproducibility.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Architectural Ambiguity**: The manuscript positions MaPLe as a baseline but omits critical implementation details. Figure 2 illustrates adapters and prompts but lacks clarity on *how* the coupling function \( F \) (mentioned in Sec. 3.1) is integrated. Additionally, visual prompts (orange blocks) are absent in the image encoder layers of Figure 2, contradicting the text (Sec. 3.2). This ambiguity complicates replication.  
2. **Limited Model Generalizability**: While CLIP (ViT-B/16) is the primary backbone, testing on newer architectures (e.g., EVA-CLIP) would bolster claims of broader applicability. Though supplementary results with EVA-CLIP (Table 10) show a similar HM gain (+1.69% over MaPLe), these are relegated to the appendix and not contextualized against contemporary methods.  
3. **Insufficient Comparison to PromptSRC**: PromptSRC (ICCV’23) also employs consistency constraints but is superficially contrasted. The manuscript notes differences (e.g., CoPrompt uses LLM perturbations and cosine loss vs. PromptSRC’s handcrafted prompts), yet Table 1 shows CoPrompt’s HM is only +0.51% higher. A deeper analysis—such as comparing parameter efficiency or cross-dataset robustness (Table 2: CoPrompt 67.0% vs. PromptSRC 65.81%)—is needed to justify novelty.  
4. **Suboptimal Figure Quality**: Figures 1–2 suffer from low resolution and confusing design. In Figure 1b, adapters are not depicted despite being central to CoPrompt. Figure 2 uses minuscule text and overlapping elements (e.g., "Adapter" labels obscured by arrows), hindering comprehension.  
5. **Terminology Inconsistencies**: Section 3 erroneously refers to adapters as "prompts" (e.g., "prompts to the text branch" vs. "adapters" in Gao et al.). This conflation risks misrepresenting contributions.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Architecture Clarity**: Could the authors clarify how the coupling function \( F \) from MaPLe is incorporated into CoPrompt’s design (Figure 2)? Additionally, should visual prompts appear in all image encoder layers per Sec. 3.1?  
2. **Model Scalability**: Would CoPrompt maintain gains on larger backbones (e.g., ViT-L/14) or other vision-language models (e.g., ALIGN)?  
3. **PromptSRC Comparison**: Can the authors expand Table 1 to include training efficiency metrics (e.g., GPU hours) and disentangle gains from LLM perturbations vs. consistency?  
4. **Figure Revisions**: High-resolution figures with explicit adapter labels and consistent color schemes are essential.  
5. **Writing Corrections**: Sec. 3’s conflation of "prompts" and "adapters" requires revision to accurately reflect the method.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper’s strengths—novel consistency framework, strong empirical results, and thorough ablations—outweigh its weaknesses. However, clarifying architectural ambiguities, expanding comparisons, and improving presentation are critical for publication.

reviews.summary: The paper presents an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate the risk of overfitting. This approach employs a consistency term applied to two altered inputs within the text and visual spheres. In text modality, the method leverages existing language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 to introduce variations, whereas for images, it uses standard image augmentation techniques prevalent in self-supervised learning. The authors have skillfully merged two distinct approaches to adaptation - prompting and adaptation - demonstrating that this synergy, coupled with a consistency loss, enhances the method's ability to generalize. The improved generalization capability of this approach is evident in various prompt learning tasks, including adapting from base to new tasks, cross-dataset evaluation, and domain generalization, with consistent enhancements observed across these applications.
reviews.strengths: The paper is clear and of high quality, with significant numerical results. The authors offer a thorough analysis of their proposed method's various components, which overall appear sensible and well-founded.
reviews.weaknesses:
While the paper is clear, and the numerical results are noteworthy, its novelty isn't entirely clear. The paper's self-consistency terms seem similar to those in self-supervised learning (SSL) methods. The authors' claim of differentiating their approach from SSL, where two perturbed inputs within a single encoder are used, doesn't fully convince. In SSL, typically there are two encoders: an online encoder and a momentum encoder. The paper’s pre-trained and trainable encoders appear analogous to SSL’s momentum and online encoders, respectively. The authors should clarify this similarity.

Additionally, the paper omits recent relevant studies like Bayesian Prompt Learning [1] and Prompt Distribution Learning [2], which address overfitting in vision and language models. Discussing these in the related work and comparing them in sections like domain generalization are necessary, especially given that in some cases, such as Bayesian Prompt Learning, they outperform the methods in this paper. For example, in the domain generalization task, the Bayesian Prompt Learning method (%60.44) works better than the paper performance (%60.42). 

[1]. Bayesian Prompt Learning for Image-Language Model Generalization, ICCV 2023

[2]. Prompt Distribution Learning, CVPR 2022
reviews.questions: Please see the weaknesses section.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.text:
### Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**  
The paper introduces CoPrompt, an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate overfitting risks. This approach applies a consistency loss to perturbed inputs in both textual and visual domains: leveraging LLMs (GPT-2/GPT-3) to diversify text prompts and employing standard image augmentations. The authors skillfully merge prompting (e.g., MaPLe) and adapter-based tuning (e.g., CLIP-Adapter), demonstrating that their synergy—enhanced by consistency constraints—boosts generalization. This is validated across three critical benchmarks: base-to-novel generalization (Table 1), cross-dataset evaluation (Table 2), and domain generalization (Table 3). CoPrompt achieves SOTA harmonic mean (80.48%) on 11 datasets, with notable gains in novel-class accuracy (+1.13% over PromptSRC) and cross-dataset performance (+0.7% over MaPLe). The ablation studies (Tables 4–7) rigorously dissect component contributions, and computational analyses (Table 8) confirm efficiency (only 3.34% more parameters than CLIP).  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Clarity and Technical Quality**: The manuscript is exceptionally well-structured, with lucid methodological explanations (Sections 3.1–3.2) and precise experimental protocols (Section 4.1). Figure 2 effectively illustrates CoPrompt’s dual-branch consistency mechanism.  
2. **Strong Empirical Results**: The comprehensive evaluations (11 datasets, 3 tasks) demonstrate CoPrompt’s robustness. Key highlights include:  
   - **Base-to-Novel Generalization**: CoPrompt outperforms 7 baselines (Table 1a), e.g., +1.93% HM over MaPLe on average and a remarkable 85.84% on EuroSAT (Table 1k).  
   - **Cross-Dataset Generalization**: CoPrompt achieves 67.0% average accuracy (Table 2), excelling on 8/10 datasets (e.g., +4.51% on EuroSAT vs. PromptSRC).  
3. **Rigorous Analysis**: Ablation studies (Tables 4–5) offer granular insights, such as the critical role of text-modality consistency (80.02% vs. 79.59% for image-only) and optimal λ=8 (Table 6). Sensitivity analyses (Tables 6–7) further validate design choices (e.g., 12 prompt layers). The appendix (Table 9) quantifies representation alignment with CLIP, reinforcing the consistency mechanism’s efficacy.  
4. **Practical Impact**: The integration of adapters and prompts resolves prior incompatibilities (Section 3.2), enabling tunable parameters without overfitting. Training efficiency (25% overhead vs. MaPLe) and code availability enhance reproducibility.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Novelty Concerns**: The core consistency mechanism bears striking resemblance to self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks. In SSL (e.g., BYOL, SimSiam), dual encoders—online (trainable) and momentum (slowly updated)—align representations of perturbed inputs. CoPrompt’s frozen pre-trained encoder and trainable encoder (Section 3.2) mirror this setup, with consistency losses (Eq. 3–5) functionally akin to SSL’s alignment objectives. The authors argue differentiation by contrasting "two learnable encoders" in SSL vs. their "learnable + frozen" pair (Section 1), but this distinction is superficial. The pre-trained encoder here parallels SSL’s momentum encoder (non-trainable, stabilized target), and the text/image perturbations align with SSL’s augmentation strategies. Without deeper architectural or theoretical differentiation, the novelty appears incremental.  
2. **Omission of Relevant Work**:  
   - **Bayesian Prompt Learning** (Derakhshani et al., ICCV 2023) formulates prompt tuning as variational inference, mitigating overfitting via probabilistic constraints. It outperforms CoPrompt in domain generalization (60.44% vs. 60.42% in Table 3), yet is omitted from discussion and Table 3 comparisons.  
   - **Prompt Distribution Learning** (Lu et al., CVPR 2022) learns soft prompts from data distributions, reducing manual bias. Its absence from Section 2 is notable, given its relevance to CoPrompt’s data-driven perturbations.  
   These omissions weaken the positioning of CoPrompt’s contributions. Including them would contextualize how consistency constraints compare to Bayesian or distributional regularization.  

**Questions:**  
1. **SSL Similarity**: Can the authors further clarify how CoPrompt’s consistency framework fundamentally differs from SSL paradigms beyond the "frozen vs. trainable" argument? For instance, does the joint use of adapters/prompts introduce a unique dynamic not found in SSL?  
2. **Comparative Analysis**: Why were Bayesian Prompt Learning and Prompt Distribution Learning excluded from evaluations? Including them in Table 3 (domain generalization) and Section 2 would strengthen the paper’s comprehensiveness.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper’s empirical strengths—significant, reproducible gains across diverse tasks (Tables 1–2) and thorough ablations—outweigh its novelty limitations. However, the resemblance to SSL and omitted comparisons slightly diminish its conceptual impact. Addressing these in revision would solidify its contribution.  

---  
*Decision*: **Accept (marginally)**

The paper presents an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate the risk of overfitting. This approach employs a consistency term applied to two altered inputs within the text and visual spheres. In text modality, the method leverages existing language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 to introduce variations, whereas for images, it uses standard image augmentation techniques prevalent in self-supervised learning. The authors have skillfully merged two distinct approaches to adaptation - prompting and adaptation - demonstrating that this synergy, coupled with a consistency loss, enhances the method's ability to generalize. The improved generalization capability of this approach is evident in various prompt learning tasks, including adapting from base to new tasks, cross-dataset evaluation, and domain generalization, with consistent enhancements observed across these applications.

Strengths:
The paper is clear and of high quality, with significant numerical results. The authors offer a thorough analysis of their proposed method's various components, which overall appear sensible and well-founded.

Weaknesses:
While the paper is clear, and the numerical results are noteworthy, its novelty isn't entirely clear. The paper's self-consistency terms seem similar to those in self-supervised learning (SSL) methods. The authors' claim of differentiating their approach from SSL, where two perturbed inputs within a single encoder are used, doesn't fully convince. In SSL, typically there are two encoders: an online encoder and a momentum encoder. The paper’s pre-trained and trainable encoders appear analogous to SSL’s momentum and online encoders, respectively. The authors should clarify this similarity.

Additionally, the paper omits recent relevant studies like Bayesian Prompt Learning [1] and Prompt Distribution Learning [2], which address overfitting in vision and language models. Discussing these in the related work and comparing them in sections like domain generalization are necessary, especially given that in some cases, such as Bayesian Prompt Learning, they outperform the methods in this paper. For example, in the domain generalization task, the Bayesian Prompt Learning method (%60.44) works better than the paper performance (%60.42).

[2]. Prompt Distribution Learning, CVPR 2022

reviews.summary: This paper presents a consistency-guided prompt learning (CoPrompt) method to transfer CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Experimental results show the capacity of consistency-guided prompt learning to imporve the generalization comparing with the SOTA methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. CoPrompt achieves the SOTA results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain setting. 

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?
reviews.questions:
1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper presents a consistency-guided prompt learning (CoPrompt) method to transfer CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Experimental results show the capacity of consistency-guided prompt learning to imporve the generalization comparing with the SOTA methods.

Strengths:
1. CoPrompt achieves the SOTA results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain setting. 

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.

Weaknesses:
1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?

Questions:
1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces Consistency-guided Prompt Learning (CoPrompt), a novel fine-tuning approach for vision-language models that aims to enhance generalization in few-shot downstream tasks. The core innovation lies in enforcing consistency constraints between the trainable model and the pre-trained CLIP backbone to mitigate overfitting, supplemented by two key components: (1) consistency applied to perturbed inputs (LLM-augmented text and augmented images) to strengthen regularization, and (2) integration of prompt tuning and adapter modules for greater flexibility in input/output space adaptation. Extensive experiments across 11 datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art (SOTA) results in base-to-novel generalization (e.g., 77.23% novel accuracy, 80.48% harmonic mean), cross-dataset evaluation (67.0% average accuracy), and competitive domain generalization. The manuscript includes rigorous ablation studies (Tables 4–7) validating each component's contribution.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **SOTA Performance:** CoPrompt achieves compelling improvements over existing methods. As shown in Table 1, it surpasses PromptSRC by 1.13% in novel-class accuracy and 0.51% in harmonic mean across 11 datasets. Notably, it excels in cross-dataset evaluation (Table 2), outperforming MaPLe by 0.70% and PromptSRC by 1.19%, highlighting its robust transferability. The integration of adapters and prompts (e.g., 4.74M new parameters) synergizes effectively with consistency constraints, enabling higher parameter counts without overfitting (Table 8).  
2. **Ablation Rigor:** The ablation studies (Tables 4–7) are thorough and methodical. Table 4 quantifies the impact of each component: removing adapters reduces HM by 0.46%, while omitting consistency constraints causes a severe 2.03% drop (due to overfitting). Tables 5a–5f further dissect design choices, revealing that text-modality consistency (80.02% HM) is more critical than image-only (79.59%), and cosine distance outperforms MSE/L1 as a constraint. These analyses solidify the method's architectural validity.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Methodological Novelty:** While the consistency mechanism is well-motivated, the core technical components—prompt tuning (MaPLe) and adapters (CLIP-Adapter)—are reused from prior work. The manuscript acknowledges this (Section 2), but the primary novelty lies in integrating these via consistency constraints rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm. For instance, the adapter design (two linear layers with non-linearity) mirrors Gao et al. (2023), and multimodal prompting builds directly on MaPLe’s coupling function (Section 3.1). The ablation in Table 11 confirms that naively combining prompts/adapters without consistency harms performance (78.45% vs. 80.48%), underscoring that the constraint is the key enabler.  
2. **Incomplete CLIP Comparison:** The zero-shot CLIP baseline (71.70% HM in Table 1a) uses hand-crafted prompts, while CoPrompt leverages LLM-augmented text (e.g., GPT-3 generating descriptive sentences like *"A four-legged animal with fur, floppy ears..."*). This asymmetry is problematic: Appendix A.5 (Table 12) shows that using LLM prompts *alone* degrades CLIP’s zero-shot accuracy to 69.14%, likely due to misalignment with CLIP’s pretraining. Thus, CoPrompt’s gains over CLIP (80.48% vs. 71.70%) partly stem from external LLM knowledge rather than solely consistency. The manuscript should disentangle these effects by including a CLIP+LLM baseline in Table 1.  
3. **Inadequate Analysis of λ Sensitivity:** For EuroSAT (Table 6), higher λ values (e.g., λ=8) reduce accuracy from 85.84% (λ=0.1) to 78.63%, suggesting dataset-specific over-regularization. However, the manuscript omits analysis of this phenomenon. As EuroSAT is small (satellite imagery) and dissimilar to CLIP’s pretraining data, aggressive consistency may suppress task-specific adaptation. The authors should investigate whether λ=0 (no consistency) or dynamic λ scheduling could improve EuroSAT results, as hinted by its outlier status in Table 6.  

**Questions:**  
1. *Could consistency constraints improve other methods like CoCoOp?* Table 13 shows that adding consistency to CoOp elevates novel-class accuracy by 1.65% (63.22%→64.87%). Extending this to CoCoOp—which conditions prompts on image inputs—may further boost generalization, potentially closing the gap with CoPrompt (e.g., CoCoOp’s 76.16% HM vs. CoPrompt’s 80.48%). Such an experiment would strengthen the generality of the consistency principle.  
2. *What is the training overhead?* Section 4.6 notes CoPrompt requires 25% more training time per epoch than MaPLe (e.g., 2:09 vs. 1:43 minutes for Flowers102 on a V100 GPU) due to dual forward passes for consistency. However, inference FLOPs match MaPLe. A clearer comparison of training costs (e.g., total GPU hours for all datasets) would help practitioners assess scalability.  

**Rating:**  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  
**Justification:** While CoPrompt delivers SOTA results and rigorous ablations, its reliance on established techniques (prompts/adapters) limits groundbreaking novelty. The CLIP comparison issue and EuroSAT sensitivity analysis are minor but notable omissions. That said, the consistency framework is well-executed and practically valuable for few-shot learning, meriting acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a consistency-guided prompt learning (CoPrompt) method to transfer CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Experimental results show the capacity of consistency-guided prompt learning to imporve the generalization comparing with the SOTA methods.

Strengths:
1. CoPrompt achieves the SOTA results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain setting. 

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.

Weaknesses:
1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?

Questions:
1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a consistency-enforced fine-tuning method for large foundation model CLIP that enables learning a new task from a few samples while maintaining the zero-shot generalizability. The proposed method incorporates the knowledge of a pretrained LLM with consistency constraints on the text branch and data augmentations on the image branch to improve the generalization further along with learnable adaptors on both image and text branches.
reviews.strengths:
- The  paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The authors have shown decent results on base-to-novel generalization.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The idea of adaptors and prompt-tuning already exist in the literature. Merely combining the two ideas seems an incremental work and not novel.
- The idea of retaining the generalizability of the CLIP using consistency loss has already been explored in the paper "Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting" (ICCV 2023) [1]. Hence,  the consistency loss doesn't contribute towards the novelty.
- The authors have not compared their approach to the above paper and there is also no reference to the paper.
- The improvements in the Domain generalization is marginal given that authors have fine-tuned the model. Same is true for cross-dataset evaluation.


[1] Muhammad Uzair Khattak, Syed Talal Wasim, Muzammal Naseer, Salman Khan, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Fahad Shahbaz Khan. Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting. ICCV 2023 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948)
